entry_requirements.age_range: 17 (with parental consent) to 42, per the Army's 2026 policy ceiling used elsewhere in this project (data/branch-profiles.json convention). No 15T-specific age restriction found this session.
entry_requirements.physical: Standard Army accession medical/fitness standards, plus normal color vision and hearing within defined thresholds (flightline/aircraft-maintenance safety-critical, per cogn-iq.org and operationmilitarykids.org). 15T also carries an Aircrew Member (crew chief) designation once qualified, which requires a flight physical — the specific flight-physical class was not independently confirmed this session.

Aviation-maintenance MOSs commonly require at least a Secret clearance for flightline/technical-manual access at many duty stations, but a 15T-specific requirement was not sourced; confirm with a recruiter or the official goarmy.com 15T page.

training_pipeline.description: Standard Army basic training common to all MOSs. (data/branch-profiles.json)

Also excludes possible Hazardous Duty Incentive Pay (HDIP, 'flight deck/aircrew pay') once a 15T soldier is designated and current as an Aircrew Member — a real, sourced-elsewhere DoD program, but a specific current dollar amount was not sourced this session, so it is omitted rather than guessed. Also excludes the DoD TSP contribution (up to 5% of base pay) and TRICARE's non-cash value.

bonuses.conditions: No current, verifiable 15T enlistment or reenlistment bonus figure was found this session. Per this project's binding constraint, Aviation Bonus / Aviation Career Incentive Pay programs are real but are set and revised QUARTERLY by branch manning needs — quoting a stale figure would be actively misleading. Army aviation maintenance MOSs (15-series, including 15T/15R/15U) have historically carried enlistment bonuses during periods of aviation-maintainer shortage, but no verifiable current-quarter amount was located within this session's tool budget. career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: E-1→E-4 in roughly 2-3 years is the Army-wide norm; E-5 (Sergeant) board eligibility typically follows at ~4-6 years of service. No 15T-specific timeline deviation was found — treat as the general Army enlisted pattern rather than a sourced 15T-specific figure.
reenlistment_decision_points: End of initial term of service (commonly a 3- or 4-year first contract)
Reclassification opportunities to airframe-specific variants (e.g., 15R AH-64 Attack Helicopter Repairer) or to the Warrant Officer Flight Training (WOFT) pipeline to become a pilot
The 20-year retention decision (pension cliff — see retirement block)
advanced_schools_or_certs: FAA Airframe & Powerplant (A&P) certificate eligibility pathway via the Joint Service Aviation Maintenance Technician Certification Council (JSAMTCC) process and Army COOL's Gap Analysis tool — NOT automatic; see credential_gap_note below.
Airframe-specific advanced schools (e.g., reclassification to 15R for AH-64) requiring additional formal training
career_progression.officer_conversion_path: Not a direct 'enlisted-to-officer' conversion, but a 15T soldier may compete for Warrant Officer Flight Training (WOFT) — passing the SIFT and WOCS selection — to become an Army Aviation Warrant Officer (see the companion 'army-aviator-warrant-officer' record in this file). This is a distinct accession pipeline, not an automatic reclassification.

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years — there is no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension for separating at 19 years or 10 years. Before 20 years, a helicopter repairer keeps every dollar of their own TSP contributions and earnings (always vested), plus DoD's automatic 1%-of-pay TSP contribution and its match (vested after 2 years of service) — all portable into a civilian 401(k)/IRA. The defined-benefit pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years, per DoD's own language. This applies identically regardless of specialty.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service (exact window and multiplier set annually per branch; Army's published active-component rate is 2.5x monthly basic pay for a 4-year additional service obligation). This is the general BRS continuation pay, distinct from any specialty-specific Aviation Bonus/ACP program (which was not sourced with a current amount this session — see bonuses block).
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. It runs on a points system (1 point/drill, 1 point/AD day, up to 15 membership points/year) toward a 20-'good year' qualifying threshold, with a non-regular pension that generally starts at age 60 (reducible, not below age 50, for qualifying post-2008 mobilized duty). Applies identically to Guard/Reserve aviation-maintenance billets.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, in exchange for reduced monthly retired pay until full Social Security retirement age (~67), when the monthly amount returns to the uncut level. Fully taxable in the year received.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility (up to 180 days of permissive duty in the final window before separation) is not specialty-restricted — subject to the same real gate as everyone else: unit commander approval, which is discretionary based on unit staffing/operational needs (data/transition.json). Aviation-maintenance-adjacent SkillBridge partners (e.g., airline MRO programs) exist in the civilian aviation-maintenance industry, but whether they are commonly available to a given soldier's location/timeline was not independently verified this session.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, a VA Benefits I course, a DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track (Higher Education, Career/Technical Training, or Entrepreneurship/Boots to Business), ending in a Capstone that certifies Career Readiness Standards are met. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry gates eligibility for nearly every benefit below — VA disability, VA healthcare, GI Bill, VA home loan, and most state veteran benefits. No 15T-specific significance beyond the general rule.
va_disability_common_claims: Cervical and lumbar spine conditions (for soldiers who also fly as designated Aircrew Members, from vibration/repetitive loading)
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an employment handicap, filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json), drawn from a separate entitlement bucket than the Post-9/11 GI Bill. No 15T-specific eligibility variance.
transition.source: data/transition.json (RUN TRANSITION, this project) — va_disability_common_claims sourced directly from that file's 'Aviation — aircrew and ground crew (flightline mechanics, ordnance, fuelers)' specialty-linked claims entry, citing chronic flightline/engine-run-up noise exposure and (for aircrew) sustained vibration/G-loading

relevant_certifications: FAA Airframe & Powerplant (A&P) certificate — NOT automatic; requires the JSAMTCC-documented military OJT-hours evaluation process plus separate FAA written, oral, and practical exams
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: THE ACUTE GAP FOR THIS CLUSTER, STATED PRECISELY: a military aircraft maintainer does NOT automatically hold an FAA Airframe & Powerplant (A&P) certificate. Simply having served in an aviation-maintenance MOS does not authorize a soldier to sit the FAA exams — the FAA counts only actual hands-on WORKING time in the specialty (not training time), evaluates each applicant's military experience individually on its own merits under FAA Order 8900.1 (using FAA Form 8610-2, Mechanic's Application), and the applicant must still pass separate FAA written, oral, and practical exams. There IS a documented conversion process — not automatic, but real and navigable: the Joint Service Aviation Maintenance Technician Certification Council (JSAMTCC) process, combined with each branch's COOL Gap Analysis tool (e.g., Navy COOL's Airframe & Powerplant credential page), maps which A&P knowledge areas a given MOS covers and which require self-study, and helps document verified OJT (working, not training) hours through military records before scheduling the FAA exam sequence.
civilian_crosswalk.source: FAA, 'Experience Requirements to Become an Aircraft Mechanic' (faa.gov/mechanics/become/experience); data/transition.json credential_gap section (this project, RUN TRANSITION), which independently sourced this exact example; BLS OEWS — Aircraft Mechanics and Service Technicians (49-3011), May 2024 reference period, percentile figures via web search synthesis of bls.gov/oes/2023/may/oes493011.htm (not independently WebFetched this session)

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart / service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. No tier below 50%. Only qualifying Title 10 active-duty days count. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving; cannot be initiated after separation. Dependent children must wait until the member has 10+ years of service to start using it.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting (credit/income/DTI) and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3% of loan amount depending on down payment and first/subsequent use, waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure service history, income, and other VA-benefit receipt — not by specialty. A 15T soldier's group assignment depends entirely on their individual disability rating and income profile, not their MOS.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property-tax exemptions, income-tax treatment, tuition waivers, etc.) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools (in-state rate applies to all users) or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a housing allowance keyed to the school's zip code and a $1,000/yr book stipend — scaled by the eligibility tier above. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving. Applies the same across all six branches per data/education-benefits.json.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Army Credentialing Assistance (CA)/Army COOL: reduced to a $2,000/fiscal-year cap in 2024, lifetime limit reported at 3 credentials per 10 years of service; commissioned officers reported ineligible. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level — cool.osd.mil could not be independently fetched this session. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: Army COOL lists the JSAMTCC-based FAA A&P certification pathway directly against 15T — this is the specialty's most concrete civilian-credentialing bridge, funded in part through Credentialing Assistance for exam fees once the JSAMTCC gap analysis and OJT documentation are complete. This is NOT automatic and requires the soldier to proactively initiate the JSAMTCC/COOL Gap Analysis process before separating.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: Deploys and trains with the supported aviation unit (e.g., a Combat Aviation Brigade); maintenance operational tempo tracks the unit's flight-hour program and deployment schedule rather than a fixed rating-specific rotation. No sourced deployment-frequency figure specific to 15T was found this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Assignments are tied to units that operate the UH-60 airframe; deployments follow the supported aviation brigade's schedule. Garrison duty involves shift work tied to flying-hour programs, which can include early/late hours around flight schedules.

entry_requirements.other: IMPORTANT SCHEMA NOTE: Warrant Officer (W-1 through W-5) is a distinct officer category from commissioned officer (O-1 through O-10). Army aviation is unusual among the six branches in that the large majority of its pilots are Warrant Officers, not commissioned officers. BINDING-CONSTRAINT NOTE: Selection for Army aviation officer (Warrant Officer) training does NOT use the AFOQT or ASTB-E named in this project's binding brief for Air Force / Navy-USMC-USCG officer aviation — the Army's own aviation-selection instrument is the SIFT (Selection Instrument for Flight Training), a separate seven-subtest aviation aptitude test. Per battalionduty.com (cross-referencing Army aviation warrant officer program materials): a SIFT score of ≥40 plus a GT (General Technical) ASVAB composite floor of 110 are required for aviation warrant officer MOS selection. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level — a direct fetch of the official Army WOFT eligibility page (recruiting.army.mil) was not completed within this session's budget. Because the GT floor is drawn from the ASVAB, entry_requirements.asvab_line_score is technically not null in practice (GT≥110 gates eligibility), but the AVIATION-SPECIFIC selection composite is the non-ASVAB SIFT — recorded here as null per the schema's intent to flag that this is not a standard ASVAB-line-score-gated MOS the way 15T is.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: PER THIS PROJECT'S BINDING CONSTRAINT, STATED PRECISELY: military pilots need FAA certificates for civilian flying — an Army Aviation Warrant Officer does NOT hold a civilian FAA pilot certificate automatically upon completing WOFT/IERW or by virtue of military flight hours alone. To fly civilian aircraft — including as an EMS, charter, agricultural, or airline pilot — a former military aviator must separately hold the applicable FAA certificate (at minimum, Commercial Pilot; Airline Transport Pilot for Part 121 airline flying) and rating, and must meet FAA medical-certificate standards, which are administered separately from military flight-physical clearance. Military flight hours can often be credited toward the FAA's civilian flight-time requirements, and some testing accommodations exist for military-trained pilots, but this is an individually-evaluated, documented conversion process — not an automatic transfer. The specific regulatory mechanism (e.g., 14 CFR 61.73, 'Military pilots or former military pilots: Special rules') was NOT independently re-verified against a primary FAA source this session and should be confirmed at faa.gov before publishing exact procedural claims to end users.

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart / service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. Applies identically regardless of specialty or officer category.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3%, waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure history, and income — not by specialty or rank.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a zip-code-keyed housing allowance and a $1,000/yr book stipend, scaled by the eligibility tier. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Army Credentialing Assistance (CA)/Army COOL: $2,000/fiscal-year cap (reduced from $4,000 in 2024); commissioned officers reported ineligible. Whether Warrant Officers are treated as 'commissioned' for CA-eligibility purposes was NOT independently confirmed this session — flagged as a gap to verify before publishing.
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: No civilian FAA pilot-certificate pathway is automatically funded the way A&P mechanic credentials are listed on Army COOL for maintenance MOSs. A separating Army aviator's most direct routes to a civilian flying job are typically (a) using GI Bill/VR&E funding toward FAA certificate add-ons (additional ratings, ATP) at a civilian flight school — GI Bill can fund FAA-approved flight training under certain conditions — or (b) direct hire by employers who count logged military flight hours toward company minimums. Neither route was independently verified to primary-source confidence this session.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: Flight-hour currency requirements mean Army aviators fly regularly even in garrison; deployment tempo follows the supported aviation unit's rotation (e.g., a Combat Aviation Brigade). No specific deployment-frequency figure was sourced this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Frequent moves tied to airframe-specific assignments and career-gate schools (WOAC, etc.); flight status requires recurring medical recertification, which can affect career/assignment timeline if not maintained.

career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: E-1→E-4 in roughly 2-3 years and E-5 board eligibility at ~4-6 years is the Navy-wide enlisted norm; no AD-specific deviation found this session.
reenlistment_decision_points: End of initial enlistment contract (commonly 4 or 6 years)
Selection of engine-family specialization (Helo/Turbojet/Turboprop) or a shore-duty technical-instructor assignment
The 20-year retention decision (pension cliff — see retirement block)
advanced_schools_or_certs: Navy COOL lists an FAA Airframe & Powerplant (A&P) credential pathway for AD, via the Mechanic (Airframe & Powerplant) COOL credential page — same JSAMTCC-based documented-OJT-hours process described in data/transition.json; NOT automatic.
Aviation Maintenance Chief Petty Officer technical-leadership track
career_progression.officer_conversion_path: Limited Duty Officer (LDO) or Chief Warrant Officer (CWO) technical-management commissioning paths exist Navy-wide for senior enlisted aviation maintainers; an AD-specific LDO community designator was not independently confirmed this session.

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years — no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension below that. TSP contributions/match are always vested (or vested after 2 years for the automatic 1%/match) and portable regardless of when a sailor separates; the pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service under standard BRS rules; exact multiplier set annually per branch. No AD-specific continuation-pay rate was identified this session.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. Points system (1 point/drill, 1 point/AD day, up to 15 membership points/year) toward a 20-'good year' threshold, non-regular pension generally starting at age 60.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, with correspondingly reduced monthly retired pay until Full Social Security Retirement Age.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility applies; unit-commander approval remains the real, discretionary gate (data/transition.json). transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, VA Benefits I course, DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track, ending in a Capstone. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry gates eligibility for nearly every benefit below. No AD-specific significance beyond the general rule.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an employment handicap, filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json).

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months, down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3%, waived for service-connected-disability compensation recipients).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure history, and income — not by rating.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure (data/veteran-benefits.json).

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a zip-code-keyed housing allowance and a $1,000/yr book stipend.
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Navy Credentialing Opportunities On-Line (COOL) funds credential exams/renewals/fees for active-duty Navy and Navy Reserve (excluding IRR/VTU); a specific dollar cap was NOT confirmed this session.
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: Navy COOL's Airframe & Powerplant credential page is directly tied to the AD rating, making this one of the more concrete COOL-documented civilian-credentialing pathways in the aviation cluster — still requires the sailor to proactively complete the JSAMTCC gap-analysis and OJT-hours documentation process before separating.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: Sea-service rating: rotates between sea/afloat duty (carrier or squadron flight-deck/hangar-bay maintenance, standard fleet underway/deployment rotations) and shore-based maintenance duty. No AD-specific deployment-length figure was independently sourced this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Sea-duty tours involve the standard Navy underway/deployment separation pattern common to carrier-based aviation ratings, alternating with more family-stable shore-duty tours.

specialties.code: 1310 (Naval Aviator, Unrestricted Line Officer); initially designated Student Naval Aviator, 1390, during flight training

entry_requirements.education: Bachelor's degree required for commissioning, via United States Naval Academy (USNA), Naval Reserve Officer Training Corps (NROTC), or Aviation Officer Candidate School (AOCS, for college graduates). This differs from Army aviation's Warrant Officer path, which does not require a bachelor's degree.
entry_requirements.age_range: UNVERIFIED — not independently confirmed this session; general Navy officer-commissioning age limits apply, with waivers reported for prior enlisted service.
entry_requirements.physical: Must pass a Class 1 Naval Aviation medical physical administered through the Naval Aerospace Medical Institute (NAMI) — materially more stringent than general Navy accession physicals. Specific vision/anthropometric standards were not independently detailed this session.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: UNVERIFIED — not independently confirmed this session, though flight-status and aircraft-systems access commonly requires at least a Secret clearance.
entry_requirements.other: BINDING-CONSTRAINT NOTE: Navy officer aviation selection uses the ASTB-E (Aviation Selection Test Battery), NOT the ASVAB — per this project's binding aviation-cluster constraint, ASTB-E is the shared Navy-Marine Corps-Coast Guard officer aviation test, distinct from the Air Force's AFOQT. Minimum competitive composite ranges reported this session (mometrix.com, frontline-forge.com): OAR ≥ 5, AQR ≥ 5, PFAR ≥ 6 (each a 1-9 stanine) for Student Naval Aviator (pilot) selection specifically; official minimum passing composites are 4 or 5 depending on program, with actual selection running higher. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level — the official MILPERSMAN 1542-010 PDF was located via search but not independently fetched this session. OCS-route applicants are initially commissioned as Ensign with designator 1390 (Student Naval Aviator) and redesignate to 1310 only upon winging.

training_pipeline.description: Fundamentals of aviation: aerodynamics, navigation, aircraft engines, meteorology, and basic flight rules/regulations, prior to any flying. (search-sourced this session; not independently fetched from a primary Navy training-command source.)

Total pipeline length from API through winging is typically ~2 years for shorter (e.g., multi-engine/helicopter) tracks and up to ~3 years for the longest (tactical jet) tracks — per bogidope.com/milpilot.com search synthesis, NOT independently confirmed against a primary Navy source this session.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: PER THIS PROJECT'S BINDING CONSTRAINT, STATED PRECISELY: military pilots need FAA certificates for civilian flying. A Naval Aviator does NOT hold a civilian FAA pilot certificate automatically upon winging or by virtue of carrier-qualification and military flight hours alone. Carrier-specific skills (arrested landings, catapult launches) have no direct civilian analog, but core instrument/multi-engine/jet flight time is generally creditable toward FAA civilian flight-time requirements under an individually-evaluated conversion process — not an automatic transfer. The specific regulatory mechanism was not independently re-verified against a primary FAA source this session; military-to-airline pilot pipelines are well-documented in the civilian aviation industry (e.g., airline-sponsored cadet/SkillBridge programs) but exact current program terms were not sourced here.

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment — easily met for a Naval Aviator, who already incurs an 8-year post-winging obligation.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3%, waived for service-connected-disability compensation recipients).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure history, and income — not by designator or rank.

education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Navy Credentialing Opportunities On-Line (COOL) funds credential exams/renewals/fees for active-duty Navy; a specific dollar cap was NOT confirmed this session. Whether FAA pilot-certificate exam fees are COOL-fundable for the 1310 community specifically was not independently verified this session.

deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Extended family separation during carrier deployments; frequent moves tied to FRS training and operational squadron changes. Flight status requires recurring medical recertification, which can affect assignment timeline if not maintained.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: GEND composite ≥ 55, per operationmilitarykids.org/mosroadmap.com (cross-referenced). GEND is built from Word Knowledge, Paragraph Comprehension, Arithmetic Reasoning, and Mathematics Knowledge.
entry_requirements.education: High school diploma or GED (Air Force general accession standard; no 1C1X1-specific deviation found this session).
entry_requirements.age_range: 17 (with parental consent) to 42, per data/branch-profiles.json convention used elsewhere in this project.
entry_requirements.physical: Color vision must be normal — NO WAIVER AVAILABLE; colorblindness is disqualifying outright. Speech must be clear and distinct in English, assessed during the MEPS physical. (bestmilitaryresume.com, cross-referenced with foreverwingman.com)
entry_requirements.security_clearance: UNVERIFIED — not independently confirmed this session, though ATC facility access commonly requires at least a Secret clearance.
entry_requirements.other: UNIQUE TO THIS AFSC IN THE AVIATION CLUSTER: an Air Force air traffic controller must earn and hold an actual FAA Air Traffic Control Specialist Certificate before being allowed to independently perform certified controller duties — per bestmilitaryresume.com, 'the candidate must be able to meet the testing and other requirements to be awarded a Federal Aviation Administration Specialist Certificate' and 'any Air Force enlisted member aspiring to be an air traffic controller must possess this certificate before being allowed to assume the duties of a certified Air Force Air Traffic Controller.' This is earned during initial technical training/certification, not automatically granted at enlistment — but unlike the aircraft-maintenance credential gap in this cluster, a fully qualified 1C1X1 controller genuinely does hold a real FAA credential by the end of qualification. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level this session (search-summarized, not independently confirmed against a primary FAA/Air Force document).

training_pipeline.location: Joint Base San Antonio-Lackland (JBSA-Lackland), San Antonio, TX
training_pipeline.description: Commonly described as ~7.5 weeks; recorded as 7 because this schema field is an integer/float. (data/branch-profiles.json)

Curriculum covers FAA regulations, ATC principles, radar operation, emergency procedures, and standard phraseology — this is the coursework leading toward the FAA Air Traffic Control Specialist Certificate referenced in entry_requirements.other. (operationmilitarykids.org, wingduty.com)

BAH is intentionally EXCLUDED (duty-station-specific). Also excludes any ATC-specific special/incentive pay, which was not sourced this session, and the DoD TSP contribution and TRICARE's non-cash value.

bonuses.conditions: No current, verifiable 1C1X1 enlistment or Selective Reenlistment Bonus (SRB) figure was found this session. Air traffic control is a chronically shortage-prone specialty across both military and civilian FAA staffing (a well-documented national ATC staffing shortage exists as of this project's 2026-07-12 retrieval date, per general news awareness, though this specific claim was not independently re-verified this session), which historically correlates with active bonus eligibility — but per this project's binding constraint, no current-quarter figure was located within budget. Verify with a recruiter or the official Air Force SRB list.

career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: General Air Force enlisted promotion pattern (E-1→E-4 in ~2-3 years, E-5 by ~5 years via the Weighted Airman Promotion System); no 1C1X1-specific deviation found this session.
reenlistment_decision_points: End of initial enlistment (commonly 4 or 6 years)
Facility-rating progression (tower vs. radar/RAPCON vs. center-equivalent facility types) and instructor/supervisor certification
The 20-year retention decision (pension cliff — see retirement block)
advanced_schools_or_certs: FAA Air Traffic Control Specialist Certificate (earned during/after technical training — see entry_requirements.other)
Facility- and position-specific certifications at each duty-station's control tower, radar facility, or equivalent
AFSC skill-level upgrade training (apprentice → journeyman → craftsman), standard AF career-field progression pattern

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years. Before 20 years, TSP contributions/match are always vested (or vested after 2 years for the automatic 1%/match) and portable; the defined-benefit pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service under standard BRS rules; exact multiplier set annually per branch. No 1C1X1-specific continuation-pay rate was identified this session.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. Points system toward a 20-'good year' threshold, non-regular pension generally starting at age 60.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, with correspondingly reduced monthly retired pay until Full Social Security Retirement Age.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility applies; unit-commander approval remains the real, discretionary gate (data/transition.json). Note that FAA hiring timelines and prior-military-controller pathways (see civilian_crosswalk) can make SkillBridge less central to this specialty's transition than for other aviation MOSs/AFSCs.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, VA Benefits I course, DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track, ending in a Capstone. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry gates eligibility for nearly every benefit below. No AFSC-specific significance beyond the general rule.
va_disability_common_claims: Hearing loss (from sustained headset/console noise exposure over a career, by analogy to the aviation cluster's general noise-exposure pattern in data/transition.json; not independently confirmed as an ATC-specific VA claims-frequency finding this session)
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an employment handicap, filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json).

example_civilian_titles: FAA Air Traffic Control Specialist (Terminal or En Route)
relevant_certifications: FAA Air Traffic Control Specialist Certificate — already earned during Air Force technical training/qualification per entry_requirements.other, making this the aviation cluster's most direct existing-credential bridge to civilian employment
Facility-specific FAA certification (still required upon FAA hire, even with prior military certification)
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: THE MOST FAVORABLE CREDENTIAL STORY IN THIS CLUSTER, STATED WITH APPROPRIATE CAUTION: unlike aircraft maintainers (no automatic A&P) and pilots (no automatic FAA pilot certificate), a fully qualified Air Force 1C1X1 controller already holds a genuine FAA Air Traffic Control Specialist Certificate earned during military technical training/certification. Per bestmilitaryresume.com (search-sourced, not independently confirmed against a primary FAA source this session), 'the FAA actively recruits former military controllers through prior experience hiring announcements, and the combination of radar proficiency, live traffic experience, and familiarity with FAA procedures makes 1C1X1 veterans some of the most competitive candidates in the pipeline.' This does NOT mean the transition is instant or automatic — FAA hiring still requires a formal application, and facility-specific certification is required again at any new FAA facility even for prior military controllers — but the underlying federal aviation credential itself is real and already held, a genuine exception to this cluster's broader credential-gap pattern.
civilian_crosswalk.source: BLS OEWS — Air Traffic Controllers (53-2021), May 2024 reference period, via web search synthesis of bls.gov/oes/2023/may/oes532021.htm; bestmilitaryresume.com (1C1X1 military-to-civilian crosswalk page, search-sourced)

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months, down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3%, waived for service-connected-disability compensation recipients).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure history, and income — not by AFSC.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure (data/veteran-benefits.json).

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a zip-code-keyed housing allowance and a $1,000/yr book stipend.
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Air Force Credentialing Assistance (via AFVEC): reported cap of up to $4,500/year. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level — cool.osd.mil could not be independently fetched this session.
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: Because the FAA Air Traffic Control Specialist Certificate is earned as part of military qualification itself, this specialty has less need for a post-separation civilian credentialing bridge than the maintenance or pilot tracks — the primary post-separation step is applying to FAA hiring announcements rather than earning a new base credential from scratch.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: Primarily a garrison/installation-based specialty (tower and radar facilities are fixed infrastructure), though deployable ATC teams support expeditionary airfields. No 1C1X1-specific deployment-frequency figure was found this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Shift-work schedule (24/7 facility staffing) is a defining lifestyle feature, more so than deployment frequency; assignments are tied to specific tower/radar facilities.

entry_requirements.education: Bachelor's degree required for commissioning. Three commissioning routes all lead to an O-1 commission: United States Air Force Academy (USAFA, apply before senior year of high school), Air Force ROTC (AFROTC) at a participating university, or Officer Training School (OTS) after graduating from college. (wingduty.com, baseops.net)

entry_requirements.physical: Must pass a Class 1 military flight physical. Specific standards were not independently detailed this session.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: UNVERIFIED — not independently confirmed this session.
entry_requirements.other: BINDING-CONSTRAINT NOTE: Air Force officer aviation selection uses the AFOQT (Air Force Officer Qualifying Test), NOT the ASVAB, per this project's binding aviation-cluster constraint. Minimum AFOQT Pilot composite score is 25; minimum Navigator-Nav composite is 10; general commissioning minimums are Verbal ≥15 and Quantitative ≥10 — but competitive selected-pilot candidates typically post Pilot-composite scores in the 60s or higher (open-exam-prep.com, afoqtprep.com, cross-referenced). Completing pilot training (UPT/SUPT) triggers a 10-year active-duty service commitment from the date of earning wings (afrotc.utk.edu, wingduty.com).

training_pipeline.location: One of five pilot-training bases: Columbus AFB MS, Laughlin AFB TX, Vance AFB OK, Sheppard AFB TX, or NAS Whiting Field FL
training_pipeline.description: Classroom instruction covering aircraft systems and basic instrument-flying procedures, before any flying begins. (baseops.net, laughlin.af.mil)
~90 flight hours in the T-6 Texan II: basic contact maneuvers/aerobatics, basic instrument flying, and basic 2-ship formation flying. (baseops.net)

Reported range is 24-28 weeks; 26 used here as a flagged midpoint. Total SUPT/UPT program length is reported at approximately 54 weeks (~12 months) end to end. (baseops.net, sofrep.com)

Also excludes Aviation Career Incentive Pay ('flight pay') and Hazardous Duty Incentive Pay, which apply once winged and were not quantified this session.

bonuses.conditions: Not applicable in the traditional sense — officers commission and incur a fixed 10-year active-duty service commitment from date of winging (afrotc.utk.edu, wingduty.com, search-sourced) rather than 'enlisting' or 'reenlisting.' The analogous retention program is Aviation Bonus / Aviation Continuation Pay (ACP), typically offered as pilots approach the end of that initial commitment. Per this project's binding constraint, ACP amounts are revised QUARTERLY (they have also been the subject of periodic congressional/DoD policy changes in recent years) and no current, verifiable figure was located within this session's budget. Verify with a recruiter or the official Air Force Aviation Bonus program page before relying on any figure.

career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: O-1→O-2 generally after ~2 years, O-2→O-3 after ~4 years (largely time-based at the junior-officer level Air Force-wide). O-4 board eligibility follows career milestones including flight-lead/instructor-pilot upgrades and, for some tracks, initial operational deployments — not independently detailed this session.
reenlistment_decision_points: The 10-year active-duty service commitment from date of winging is the pilot community's analogous 'decision point' to enlisted reenlistment

The 20-year retention decision (pension cliff — see retirement block)
advanced_schools_or_certs: Formal Training Unit (FTU) / platform-specific qualification course upon UPT graduation, before reporting to an operational squadron (not independently detailed this session)
USAF Weapons School (competitive, advanced tactics instructor qualification) for select platforms/pilots

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years, identically for pilots as for any other officer or enlisted member. Before 20 years, TSP contributions/match are portable and vested; the pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years. Note the pilot's own 10-year post-winging service commitment is separate from, and shorter than, the 20-year pension cliff.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service under standard BRS rules. Distinct from the aviation-specific Aviation Bonus/ACP program described in the bonuses block above, which has its own separate timing tied to the 10-year post-winging service commitment.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. Points system toward a 20-'good year' threshold, non-regular pension generally starting at age 60. Air National Guard fighter/airlift squadrons are a well-known path for prior-active-duty pilots to continue flying in a Guard status — not independently detailed this session.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, with correspondingly reduced monthly retired pay until Full Social Security Retirement Age.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility applies to officers as well as enlisted members; unit-commander approval remains the real, discretionary gate (data/transition.json). Airline-industry SkillBridge and cadet-pipeline partnerships (regional and major carriers) are a prominent, well-documented feature of the military-pilot-to-airline transition, but specific current program terms were not independently sourced this session.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, VA Benefits I course, DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track, ending in a Capstone. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry gates eligibility for nearly every benefit below. No specialty-specific significance beyond the general rule.

Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json).

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: PER THIS PROJECT'S BINDING CONSTRAINT, STATED PRECISELY: military pilots need FAA certificates for civilian flying. An Air Force pilot does NOT hold a civilian FAA pilot certificate automatically upon completing SUPT/UPT or by virtue of military flight hours and type qualification alone. To fly civilian aircraft — including as an airline, charter, or corporate pilot — a former Air Force pilot must separately hold the applicable FAA certificate (at minimum, Commercial Pilot; Airline Transport Pilot for Part 121 airline flying) and rating, and must meet FAA medical-certificate standards administered separately from military flight-physical clearance. Military flight hours can often be credited toward FAA civilian flight-time requirements, and some testing accommodations exist for military-trained pilots, but this is an individually-evaluated, documented conversion process — not an automatic transfer. The specific regulatory mechanism (e.g., 14 CFR 61.73) was NOT independently re-verified against a primary FAA source this session and should be confirmed at faa.gov before publishing exact procedural claims to end users. The military-to-airline pilot pipeline is well-established in the U.S. aviation industry (airline cadet programs actively recruit separating military pilots), but current program terms change and were not sourced here.
civilian_crosswalk.source: BLS OEWS — Commercial Pilots (53-2012) and Airline Pilots, Copilots, and Flight Engineers (53-2011), May 2024 reference period, via web search synthesis; general binding-constraint framing from this task's own instructions

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months, down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment — easily met for an Air Force pilot, who already incurs a 10-year post-winging commitment.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3%, waived for service-connected-disability compensation recipients).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure history, and income — not by AFSC or rank.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure (data/veteran-benefits.json).

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a zip-code-keyed housing allowance and a $1,000/yr book stipend.
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Air Force Credentialing Assistance (via AFVEC): reported cap of up to $4,500/year. Whether FAA pilot-certificate exam fees are AFVEC/CA-fundable was not independently verified this session.
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: GI Bill funding can, under certain conditions, apply toward FAA-approved flight training/type-rating add-ons at a civilian flight school — not independently verified to primary-source confidence this session. Airline cadet and SkillBridge partnership programs are a well-known bridge for separating Air Force pilots, but specific current program terms were not sourced here.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: Deployment tempo follows the Air Expeditionary Force rotation model for the pilot's assigned platform/squadron. No specific deployment-frequency figure was independently sourced this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Frequent moves tied to FTU training and operational squadron changes; flight status requires recurring medical recertification, which can affect assignment timeline if not maintained. Many fighter/airlift pilots transition to Air National Guard or Reserve units after their active-duty commitment to continue flying part-time alongside a civilian airline career.
